---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
# bibliography: references.bib
csl: ieee.csl
---

# Logistic-Normal Multinomial model {#sec-logistic-normal-multinomial}

In @sec-dirichlet-multinomial, we derived the factorization of a joint
distribution of independent negative binomial random variables with shared
$\hat{p}$ into the product of a negative binomial for the total count and a
Dirichlet-Multinomial for the composition:

$$
\pi(\underline{u} \mid \underline{r}, \hat{p}) =
\underbrace{
\pi\left(u_T \mid r_T, \hat{p}\right)
}_{\text{Negative Binomial}}
\times
\underbrace{
\pi(\underline{u} \mid u_T, \underline{r})
}_{\text{Dirichlet-Multinomial}}.
$${#eq-lnm-dm-recap}

This factorization is elegant and computationally convenient: the
Dirichlet-Multinomial integral has a closed form (see
@eq-joint-target-int-dirichlet-multinomial), and the compositional distribution
depends only on the concentration parameters $\underline{r}$, not on
$\hat{p}$. However, it carries a fundamental limitation for cross-gene
correlation analysis: **the correlation structure of the compositions
$\underline{\rho}$ is entirely determined by the concentration parameters
$\underline{r}$, with no additional degrees of freedom for modeling direct
gene-gene dependencies.**

In this section, we develop an alternative compositional model---the
**Logistic-Normal Multinomial** (LNM)---that replaces the Dirichlet prior on
compositions with a logistic-normal distribution. This substitution introduces
an explicit, parameterizable covariance structure in log-ratio space, enabling
the model to represent arbitrary cross-gene correlations. Beyond the statistical
motivation, the logistic-normal has a direct biophysical justification: it is
the steady-state distribution of a system of interacting genes under the linear
noise approximation, with the covariance determined by the gene regulatory
network's Jacobian (see @sec-lnm-biophysical). The trade-off is that
the Dirichlet-Multinomial's closed-form integral is lost, requiring approximate
inference techniques for the per-cell composition latent variable.

## Limitations of the Dirichlet for correlation modeling {#sec-lnm-dirichlet-limitations}

To understand why the Dirichlet distribution is insufficient for modeling
cross-gene correlations, recall from @sec-dirichlet-multinomial that the
Dirichlet-Multinomial arises from integrating out a Dirichlet-distributed
composition vector,

$$
\underline{\rho} \mid \underline{r} \sim
\text{Dirichlet}(\underline{r}),
$${#eq-lnm-dirichlet-prior}

over a multinomial likelihood. The Dirichlet distribution on the $G$-simplex
has $G$ parameters (the concentrations $r_1, \ldots, r_G$), and its covariance
structure is fully determined by these concentrations. Specifically, the
covariance between components $\rho_g$ and $\rho_{g'}$ for $g \neq g'$ is

$$
\text{Cov}(\rho_g, \rho_{g'}) =
\frac{-r_g r_{g'}}{r_T^2 (r_T + 1)},
$${#eq-lnm-dirichlet-covariance}

where $r_T = \sum_{j=1}^G r_j$. Two properties of @eq-lnm-dirichlet-covariance
are important:

1. **All pairwise covariances are negative.** This is a structural property of
   the Dirichlet: since the components must sum to one, increasing one
   component necessarily decreases others, forcing all pairwise covariances to
   be non-positive. There is no way to make two components positively
   correlated under a Dirichlet distribution.

2. **The correlation matrix is fully determined by $\underline{r}$.** Given the
   $G$ concentration parameters, there are no free parameters left to tune the
   correlation structure. The normalized correlation between $\rho_g$ and
   $\rho_{g'}$ is

$$
\text{Corr}(\rho_g, \rho_{g'}) =
\frac{-\sqrt{r_g r_{g'}}}{\sqrt{(r_T - r_g)(r_T - r_{g'})}},
$${#eq-lnm-dirichlet-correlation}

which depends only on the ratios $r_g / r_T$.

These properties mean that even with a low-rank variational guide that learns
correlations in the posterior over $\underline{r}$ (as used in the standard
SCRIBE model), the generative model itself cannot express cross-gene
correlations beyond those implicitly imposed by the simplex constraint. As
demonstrated in our synthetic validation experiments, the low-rank guide's $W$
factor captures **likelihood-induced coupling**---generic posterior
correlations arising from shared data and model structure---which overwhelms
any true generative correlation signal and makes direct extraction of
gene-gene dependencies unreliable. This statistical limitation also
has a biophysical counterpart: the Dirichlet arises from the assumption
that genes transcribe independently, whereas real genes form regulatory
networks that induce the very correlations the Dirichlet cannot represent
(see @sec-lnm-biophysical for the full derivation).

### In log-ratio space, the limitation is even clearer {#sec-lnm-dirichlet-logr}

Consider the centered log-ratio (CLR) transformation of a
Dirichlet-distributed vector. If $\underline{\rho} \sim
\text{Dirichlet}(\underline{r})$, the CLR coordinates

$$
z_{g,\text{CLR}} = \log \rho_g - \frac{1}{G} \sum_{j=1}^G \log \rho_j
$${#eq-lnm-clr-def}

have a distribution that is determined entirely by $\underline{r}$. There are
no additional parameters to control the covariance of
$\underline{z}_{\text{CLR}}$.

This is precisely the gap that the logistic-normal fills: it places a Gaussian
distribution in log-ratio space with an explicit covariance matrix, providing
the missing degrees of freedom.

## The logistic-normal distribution {#sec-lnm-logistic-normal}

The logistic-normal distribution is a probability distribution on the simplex
obtained by applying the inverse additive log-ratio (ALR) transformation to a
multivariate normal random variable. We define it in three steps.

### The ALR transformation {#sec-lnm-alr}

The **additive log-ratio** (ALR) transformation maps a point on the
$G$-simplex to $\mathbb{R}^{G-1}$ by taking log-ratios with respect to a
reference component (conventionally the last one):

$$
z_g = \log \frac{\rho_g}{\rho_G}, \quad g = 1, 2, \ldots, G-1,
$${#eq-lnm-alr-forward}

where $\underline{\rho} = (\rho_1, \ldots, \rho_G)$ satisfies $\rho_g > 0$ and
$\sum_{g=1}^G \rho_g = 1$. The inverse transformation recovers the simplex point
from the log-ratios:

$$
\rho_g = \frac{\exp(z_g)}{1 + \sum_{j=1}^{G-1} \exp(z_j)},
\quad g = 1, \ldots, G-1,
$${#eq-lnm-alr-inverse-first}

$$
\rho_G = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{j=1}^{G-1} \exp(z_j)}.
$${#eq-lnm-alr-inverse-last}

This is a bijection between the interior of the $G$-simplex and
$\mathbb{R}^{G-1}$. The ALR is closely related to the CLR used in
@sec-diffexp-gaussianity. Given ALR coordinates $\underline{z} \in
\mathbb{R}^{G-1}$, the CLR coordinates are obtained by the linear centering
operation (see @sec-lnm-alr-to-clr below).

### Definition of the logistic-normal {#sec-lnm-logistic-normal-def}

A random vector $\underline{\rho}$ on the $G$-simplex follows a **logistic-normal
distribution** if its ALR transform $\underline{z}$ follows a multivariate
normal distribution:

$$
\underline{z} \sim
\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}),
\quad \underline{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1},
$${#eq-lnm-base-mvn}

$$
\underline{\rho} = \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}),
\quad \underline{\rho} \in \Delta^G,
$${#eq-lnm-simplex-map}

where $\underline{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$ is the location (mean
composition in log-ratio space) and $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ is a
$(G-1) \times (G-1)$ positive-definite covariance matrix. We write

$$
\underline{\rho} \sim
\text{LogisticNormal}(\underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}).
$${#eq-lnm-distribution-def}

### Density on the simplex {#sec-lnm-density}

The density of the logistic-normal with respect to Lebesgue measure on the
simplex is obtained by applying the change-of-variables formula to the
multivariate normal density. We need the Jacobian of the ALR transformation.

The ALR maps $\underline{\rho} \in \Delta^G$ to
$\underline{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$ via $z_g = \log(\rho_g / \rho_G)$. The
Jacobian matrix of this transformation has entries

$$
\frac{\partial z_g}{\partial \rho_j} =
\begin{cases}
1 / \rho_g & \text{if } j = g, \\
0 & \text{if } j \neq g \text{ and } j < G.
\end{cases}
$${#eq-lnm-jacobian-entries}

However, the simplex constraint $\rho_G = 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{G-1} \rho_j$ introduces
additional dependencies. Taking into account the constraint (and applying the
standard change-of-variables formula for the simplex-to-$\mathbb{R}^{G-1}$
map), the log-determinant of the Jacobian evaluates to

$$
\log \left| \det
\underline{\underline{J}} \right| =
-\sum_{g=1}^{G} \log \rho_g.
$${#eq-lnm-log-det-jacobian}

The proof of @eq-lnm-log-det-jacobian follows from recognizing that the
Jacobian of the **inverse** ALR (from $\underline{z}$ to $\underline{\rho}$) is a
softmax Jacobian, whose log-determinant is $\sum_{g=1}^G \log \rho_g$. Inverting
gives the sign flip.

Combining the multivariate normal density with the Jacobian correction, the
logistic-normal density on the simplex is

$$
\pi(\underline{\rho} \mid \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}) =
\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{(G-1)/2}
|\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}|^{1/2}}
\exp\!\left(
-\frac{1}{2}
(\underline{z} - \underline{\mu})^\top
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}
(\underline{z} - \underline{\mu})
\right)
\prod_{g=1}^{G} \frac{1}{\rho_g},
$${#eq-lnm-density}

where $\underline{z}$ is the ALR transform of $\underline{\rho}$, i.e.,
$z_g = \log(\rho_g / \rho_G)$ for $g = 1, \ldots, G-1$.

Equivalently, in log-probability form:

$$
\log \pi(\underline{\rho} \mid \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}) =
\log \mathcal{N}(\underline{z} \mid \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
- \sum_{g=1}^{G} \log \rho_g.
$${#eq-lnm-log-density}

The first term is the standard multivariate normal log-density evaluated at the
ALR coordinates. The second term is the Jacobian correction from the
change of variables.

### Connection to CLR coordinates {#sec-lnm-alr-to-clr}

The ALR transformation treats the last component $\rho_G$ as a reference, which
introduces an asymmetry: permuting the gene labels changes the distribution.
The **centered log-ratio** (CLR) transformation removes this asymmetry by
centering:

$$
z_{g,\text{CLR}} = \log \rho_g - \frac{1}{G} \sum_{j=1}^G \log \rho_j,
\quad g = 1, \ldots, G.
$${#eq-lnm-clr-from-simplex}

Given ALR coordinates $\underline{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$, the CLR
coordinates are obtained by the linear transformation

$$
\underline{z}_{\text{CLR}} =
\underline{\underline{A}}\, \underline{z},
$${#eq-lnm-alr-to-clr}

where $\underline{\underline{A}}$ is a $G \times (G-1)$ matrix (see the
detailed derivation in the ALR-to-CLR transformation used in the differential
expression framework). Since $\underline{z}$ is Gaussian under the
logistic-normal, $\underline{z}_{\text{CLR}}$ is also Gaussian:

$$
\underline{z}_{\text{CLR}} \sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\underline{\underline{A}}\,\underline{\mu},\;
\underline{\underline{A}}\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}\,
\underline{\underline{A}}^\top
\right).
$${#eq-lnm-clr-gaussian}

This is the property that connects the logistic-normal model to the
Gaussianity diagnostics in @sec-diffexp-gaussianity: under the logistic-normal,
the CLR coordinates are **exactly** Gaussian by construction, rather than
approximately Gaussian as tested by the Jarque-Bera diagnostic.

## Equivalent parameterisations: ALR, CLR, and softmax-normal {#sec-lnm-equivalent-params}

The logistic-normal distribution defined in @sec-lnm-logistic-normal-def
is a probability distribution on the simplex $\Delta^{G-1}$, but it
admits several equivalent parameterisations. Each corresponds to a
different convention for removing the one redundant degree of freedom
inherent to compositional data: the simplex is a $(G-1)$-dimensional
manifold embedded in $\mathbb{R}^G$, so any natural ambient
representation will overcount dimensions by one. Because the
biophysical derivation of @sec-lnm-biophysical naturally produces a
$G$-dimensional Gaussian on log-abundances (not a $(G-1)$-dimensional
ALR object), and because the chapter on post-hoc fitting
(@sec-normalization) is restricted to the ALR form for reasons that do
not carry over to the present setting, we collect the three relevant
parameterisations here and clarify which simplex distribution they
each describe.

### The three parameterisations

Let $\underline{x} \in \mathbb{R}^G$ denote log-abundances, as produced
by the LNA. The composition is $\underline{\rho} =
\text{softmax}(\underline{x}) \in \Delta^{G-1}$. Three equivalent
parameterisations of the resulting distribution on $\underline{\rho}$
are:

**ALR ($G - 1$ dims, asymmetric, full-rank covariance).** Work with
the contrasts $\underline{z}_{\text{ALR}} = (x_1 - x_G,\,\ldots,\,x_{G-1} - x_G)
\in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$. Place
$$
\underline{z}_{\text{ALR}} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu}_{\text{ALR}},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{ALR}}),
$${#eq-lnm-param-alr}
with $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{ALR}}$ full-rank in
$\mathbb{R}^{(G-1)\times(G-1)}$. This is the formulation used in the
rest of this chapter.

**CLR ($G$ dims, symmetric, rank-deficient covariance).** Work with
the centered log-abundances $\underline{z}_{\text{CLR}} = \underline{x} -
\bar{x}\,\underline{1}$, where $\bar{x} = G^{-1}\sum_g x_g$. Place
$$
\underline{z}_{\text{CLR}} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu}_{\text{CLR}},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{CLR}}),
$${#eq-lnm-param-clr}
with the constraint $\underline{1}^\top \underline{\mu}_{\text{CLR}}
= 0$ and $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{CLR}}\,\underline{1} =
\underline{0}$. The covariance is rank-deficient (rank $G-1$ in $G$
dims), with $\underline{1}$ in its null space.

**Unconstrained softmax-normal ($G$ dims, symmetric, with gauge
freedom).** Work directly with $\underline{x} \in \mathbb{R}^G$
without any constraint. Place
$$
\underline{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu}_{\text{full}},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{full}}).
$${#eq-lnm-param-softmax}
The simplex distribution is invariant under
$\underline{\mu}_{\text{full}} \to \underline{\mu}_{\text{full}} + c\,
\underline{1}$, so this parameterisation has a one-dimensional
**gauge freedom**: parameters along the $\underline{1}$ direction are
not determined by any data.

### These describe the same simplex distribution

The three forms are linked by linear maps. Let $\underline{\underline{A}}
\in \mathbb{R}^{(G-1) \times G}$ be the contrast matrix from
@eq-lnm-alr-forward and $\underline{\underline{H}} =
\underline{\underline{I}}_G - G^{-1}\,\underline{1}\,\underline{1}^\top$
be the centering projector. Then:

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{ALR}} =
\underline{\underline{A}}\,\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{full}}\,
\underline{\underline{A}}^\top,
\qquad
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{CLR}} =
\underline{\underline{H}}\,\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_{\text{full}}\,
\underline{\underline{H}}^\top.
$${#eq-lnm-param-equivalence}

Both maps are many-to-one (the ALR map collapses one dimension; the
CLR projector annihilates the $\underline{1}$ direction), reflecting
the gauge freedom. The induced distribution on $\underline{\rho}$ is
the same in all three cases.

### Why ALR for this chapter

The three parameterisations are *generatively* equivalent but differ
in their fit to standard variational machinery:

| Parameterisation | Covariance rank | Constraints to enforce | Density on simplex | Variational machinery |
|------------------|-----------------|------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| ALR | Full | None | Closed form via Jacobian | `LowRankMVN` direct |
| CLR | $G - 1$ in $G$ dims | $\sum_g \mu_g = 0$, $\Sigma\,\underline{1} = \underline{0}$ | Closed form via pseudoinverse | Requires manual projection |
| Softmax-normal | Full | None | Not well-defined (gauge freedom) | Optimizer drifts along $\underline{1}$ |

For the **inference machinery** of @sec-lnm-strategy-a, all three are
in principle usable: the ELBO never requires evaluating
$\pi(\underline{\rho})$ as a density on the simplex (the multinomial
likelihood treats $\underline{\rho}$ as a parameter, not a random
variable with a density), so the singular-Jacobian obstruction that
applies to the post-hoc fitting in @sec-normalization does not
apply here. What does matter is operational simplicity:

- **ALR** has full-rank covariance, no constraints, and integrates
  directly with NumPyro's `LowRankMultivariateNormal`. It is the
  lowest-friction choice.
- **CLR** is more interpretable (symmetric across genes, matches the
  CLR coordinates used in the differential expression pipeline of
  @sec-diffexp-gaussianity) but requires either projection
  steps after each gradient update or a pseudoinverse-aware Gaussian
  density implementation.
- **Unconstrained softmax-normal** is the most natural fit to the
  biophysics but introduces a one-dimensional unidentified direction
  along which the optimizer wanders, slowing convergence and
  producing parameters that are interpretable only modulo the gauge.
  A soft penalty of the form $\lambda\,(\underline{1}^\top
  \underline{\mu})^2$ can fix the gauge stochastically, but it is
  just a softer version of what ALR/CLR enforce by construction.

We adopt the **ALR parameterisation** for the rest of the chapter on
the basis of these computational considerations. The choice does not
affect:

1. The simplex distribution being modelled (identical in all three
   forms, as established by @eq-lnm-param-equivalence).
2. The biophysical interpretation of the rank-$k$ structure (the
   column space of the loading matrix is gauge-invariant up to a
   known linear map).
3. The multinomial likelihood, the ELBO, or any quantity intrinsic
   to compositions.

After fitting, the ALR loading matrix can be translated into CLR
coordinates --- which are typically the more natural ones for biological
interpretation, since they treat all genes symmetrically --- via

$$
\underline{\underline{W}}_{\text{CLR}} = \underline{\underline{H}}\,
\begin{bmatrix}
\underline{\underline{W}}_{\text{ALR}} \\ \underline{0}^\top
\end{bmatrix},
$${#eq-lnm-W-alr-to-clr}

i.e., extending $\underline{\underline{W}}_{\text{ALR}}$ with a
zero row for the reference gene and then row-centering. Clustering on
the rows of $\underline{\underline{W}}_{\text{CLR}}$ provides a
gauge-symmetric grouping of genes by shared regulatory program,
removing the asymmetry inherited from the choice of reference gene
in the ALR step.

In short: **the ALR is best understood as a gauge-fixing convention,
not as a substantive modelling decision.** The biophysics, the
generative model, and the simplex distribution are unchanged under
the equivalent CLR or unconstrained softmax-normal forms; only the
operational cost of fitting differs.

## Contrasting the Dirichlet and the logistic-normal {#sec-lnm-contrast}

Before developing the full model, it is instructive to compare the two
distributions on the simplex side by side.

### Parameter count and correlation freedom

The Dirichlet distribution $\text{Dir}(\underline{r})$ on the $G$-simplex has
$G$ parameters (the concentrations $r_1, \ldots, r_G$). These $G$ parameters
control both the mean composition and the covariance structure simultaneously,
leaving zero free parameters for the correlation structure once the mean is
fixed.

The logistic-normal
$\text{LogisticNormal}(\underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ has $G - 1$ location parameters and
$\binom{G}{2}$ covariance parameters (the upper triangle of
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$, including diagonal entries). The total
parameter count is

$$
(G - 1) + \frac{(G-1)G}{2} = \frac{(G-1)(G+2)}{2}.
$${#eq-lnm-param-count}

For $G = 20{,}000$ genes, storing the full covariance matrix is prohibitive
($\sim 10^8$ entries). The low-rank factorization addresses this:

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d}),
$${#eq-lnm-low-rank-cov}

where $\underline{\underline{W}}$ is $(G-1) \times k$ and $\underline{d}$ is a
$(G-1)$-vector of positive values. This requires $(G-1)(k+1)$ parameters---
linear in $G$ for fixed rank $k$. The rank $k$ controls the expressiveness of
the cross-gene correlations: $k = 0$ gives a diagonal covariance (independent
genes in log-ratio space), while $k = G - 1$ recovers the full covariance.

### Correlation structure

| Property | Dirichlet | Logistic-Normal |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Support | $G$-simplex | $G$-simplex |
| Parameters | $G$ concentrations | $(G-1)$ location + $(G-1)(k+1)$ covariance |
| Pairwise correlations | Always negative | Arbitrary sign in log-ratio space |
| Correlation freedom | 0 (fixed by concentrations) | $\binom{G-1}{2}$ (full) or $O(Gk)$ (low-rank) |
| Closed-form marginals | Yes (Beta) | No (requires numerical integration) |
| DM integral | Closed form | No closed form |

The critical row is the last one: the Dirichlet-Multinomial integral has a
closed form, but the logistic-normal-multinomial does not. This is the
fundamental trade-off between the two approaches.

## Biophysical justification: steady-state gene regulatory networks {#sec-lnm-biophysical}

The preceding section established a *statistical* motivation for the
logistic-normal: the Dirichlet has zero free correlation parameters,
while the logistic-normal has $O(G^2)$ (or $O(Gk)$ in low-rank form).
This section summarizes the *biophysical* motivation developed in full
detail in @sec-lnm-biophysics. We show that the logistic-normal on gene
compositions is the natural steady-state distribution of a system of
interacting genes under standard stochastic gene expression theory,
whereas the Dirichlet-Multinomial arises only under the assumption that
genes are transcriptionally independent.

### The effective Poisson rate and its dynamics

A key conceptual result developed in @sec-lnm-bio-bursty is the precise
identity of the variable $\lambda_g$ that appears in both models.
Starting from the one-state bursty promoter---a constitutively active
promoter producing mRNA in geometrically-distributed bursts---the
effective Poisson rate $\lambda_g$ is the **expected mRNA abundance**,
and its exact steady-state distribution is a Gamma
(@sec-lnm-bio-bursty-gamma).

The dynamics of $\lambda_g$ follow from **mass balance**
(@sec-lnm-bio-mass-balance): the rate of change of the mRNA pool equals
production minus first-order degradation,

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} = k_{i,g} \cdot b_g - \gamma_g \lambda_g,
$${#eq-lnm-bio-lambda-ode-summary}

where $k_{i,g}$ is the burst initiation rate, $b_g$ the mean burst
size, and $\gamma_g$ the mRNA degradation rate. Every term has
unambiguous physical meaning: $k_{i,g} \cdot b_g$ is the mean mRNA
production rate, and $\gamma_g \lambda_g$ is first-order mRNA
degradation---the same $\gamma$ that appears in every birth-death model
of gene expression.

This resolves a potential confusion: $\lambda_g$ is **not** a
"transcription rate" (a kinetic parameter with units molecules/time),
but rather the **expected mRNA abundance** conditional on the regulatory
state. The full derivation is given in @sec-lnm-bio-lambda-def.

### Gene regulatory coupling

In a gene regulatory network (GRN), the burst initiation rate of gene
$g$ is no longer autonomous: it depends on the concentrations of
transcription factors encoded by other genes. Making the burst rate a
function of the expression vector,
$k_{i,g} \to k_{i,g}(\underline{\lambda})$
(see @sec-lnm-bio-grn-coupling), yields the coupled ODE system

$$
\frac{d\lambda_g}{dt} =
\underbrace{k_{i,g}(\underline{\lambda}) \cdot
b_g}_{b_g(\underline{\lambda})}
- \gamma_g \lambda_g,
\quad g = 1, \ldots, G.
$${#eq-lnm-bio-grn-ode}

The production term $b_g(\underline{\lambda})$ encodes the full
regulatory logic of the network. The degradation term
$\gamma_g \lambda_g$ is the same mRNA degradation as in the single-gene
case---it does not change upon introducing gene-gene interactions. This
derivation rests on several explicit assumptions (fluid limit,
protein-tracks-mRNA proportionality, constant burst size, and
first-order degradation), which are enumerated and discussed in
@sec-lnm-bio-assumptions.

### From coupled stochastic dynamics to the logistic-normal

The derivation then proceeds through a standard chain of approximations,
each developed in full in @sec-lnm-biophysics:

1. **Chemical Langevin Equation** (@sec-lnm-bio-cle): stochastic noise
   is added to the coupled ODE, modeling intrinsic gene expression
   fluctuations.
2. **Log-space transformation** (@sec-lnm-bio-log-space): Itô's lemma
   transforms the CLE to log-abundance coordinates $x_g = \log \lambda_g$.
3. **Linear Noise Approximation** (@sec-lnm-bio-lna): the drift is
   linearized around the deterministic steady state, and the noise
   amplitude is frozen at its steady-state value. The result is a
   multivariate Ornstein--Uhlenbeck (OU) process in log-space.
4. **Lyapunov equation** (@sec-lnm-bio-lyapunov): the OU steady-state
   distribution is a multivariate Gaussian,
   $\underline{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{\bar{x}},
   \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$, where the covariance
   $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ satisfies the continuous Lyapunov
   equation
   $\underline{\underline{J}}\,\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} +
   \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}\,\underline{\underline{J}}^\top +
   2\underline{\underline{D}} = 0$.
5. **Softmax to compositions** (@sec-lnm-bio-to-ln): since
   $\underline{x}$ is Gaussian, the gene composition
   $\underline{\rho} = \text{softmax}(\underline{x})$ follows a
   logistic-normal distribution by definition.

The central result is: **the logistic-normal on gene compositions is the
steady-state distribution of a gene regulatory network under the linear
noise approximation in log-abundance space.** The covariance
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ is the Lyapunov solution encoding the
regulatory interactions in $\underline{\underline{J}}$ and the
bursty-transcription noise in $\underline{\underline{D}}$.

### Universality across microscopic models

The GRN ODE above is not specific to the one-state bursty promoter.
As argued deductively in @sec-lnm-bio-deductive, the form
$\dot{\lambda}_g = b_g(\underline{\lambda}) - \gamma_g \lambda_g$ is a
necessary consequence of two physical ingredients: first-order mRNA
degradation and coarse-grainable production. In
@sec-lnm-bio-two-state, we verify this claim explicitly by deriving the
same ODE from the **two-state promoter** (promoter ON/OFF switching),
a completely different microscopic model. The microscopic model enters
only through the noise amplitude, not through the form of the result.

### The low-rank covariance as regulatory architecture

The low-rank factorization
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} = \underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \text{diag}(\underline{d})$ from
@eq-lnm-low-rank-cov has a natural biophysical interpretation
(@sec-lnm-bio-low-rank): the columns of $\underline{\underline{W}}$
approximate the dominant transcription-factor programs driving
compositional variance, while $\underline{d}$ captures gene-intrinsic
noise. This low-rank structure is not merely a computational convenience
for handling $G = 20{,}000$ genes---it reflects the biological reality
that a small number of master regulators control large downstream
programs. Note that $\underline{\underline{W}}$ is identifiable only up
to rotation, so its columns define a regulatory *subspace* rather than
individually named programs.

### Connecting the two models

The DM and LNM arise from different approximation regimes applied to the
same underlying stochastic gene expression process
(@sec-lnm-bio-connection):

| Model | Biophysical assumption | Steady-state | Correlation structure |
|-------|------------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| DM | Independent promoters ($\underline{\underline{J}}$ diagonal) | Product of Gammas (exact) | Fixed by $\underline{r}$; always negative on simplex |
| LNM | Interacting genes ($\underline{\underline{J}}$ general) | Multivariate Gaussian in log-space (LNA) | Free $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$, determined by GRN via Lyapunov |

The LNM trades exactness in the non-interacting, low-expression regime
against expressivity in the coupled regime. For genes that are both
weakly coupled and lowly expressed, the DM remains the better marginal
model. The LNM is a generalization only in the sense of admitting a
broader class of covariance structures, not in the sense of being a
uniformly better description of the underlying biophysics.

## The Logistic-Normal Multinomial model {#sec-lnm-model}

We now define the complete generative model that replaces the
Dirichlet-Multinomial component of @eq-lnm-dm-recap with a logistic-normal
composition model.

### Generative process for a single cell {#sec-lnm-generative}

The generative process for the UMI counts of a single cell $c$ proceeds in
three steps.

**Step 1: Total count.** The total UMI count is drawn from a negative binomial
distribution, exactly as in the standard model:

$$
u_T^{(c)} \sim \text{NB}(r_T, \hat{p}).
$${#eq-lnm-total-count}

This component is identical to the first factor in @eq-lnm-dm-recap and is
unchanged from the Dirichlet-Multinomial model.

**Step 2: Composition in log-ratio space.** Each cell draws its own composition
latent in ALR space from the population distribution:

$$
\underline{z}^{(c)} \sim
\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}),
$${#eq-lnm-cell-latent}

where $\underline{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$ is the population mean
composition in log-ratio space, and $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d})$ is the population covariance. These are **shared
parameters** (constant across cells), analogous to $\underline{r}$ in the
Dirichlet model. The per-cell latent $\underline{z}^{(c)}$ plays the role that
the Dirichlet draw $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} \sim
\text{Dir}(\underline{r})$ plays in the standard model: it represents the
cell-specific composition before multinomial sampling.

**Step 3: Count allocation.** The ALR latent is mapped to the simplex and used
to allocate the total count across genes:

$$
\underline{\rho}^{(c)} =
\text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}^{(c)}),
$${#eq-lnm-cell-composition}

$$
\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\, \underline{\rho}^{(c)} \sim
\text{Multinomial}(u_T^{(c)},\, \underline{\rho}^{(c)}).
$${#eq-lnm-multinomial}

**Note: scale--composition decoupling is a modeling choice.** The
generative process above factorizes the per-cell count vector into an
independent total $u_T^{(c)} \sim \text{NB}(r_T, \hat p)$ and an
independent compositional latent $\underline{z}^{(c)} \sim
\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\,\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$. This is
the same factorization that the DM uses, and it inherits the same
limitation: it is **not** strictly derivable from the GRN biophysics of
@sec-lnm-biophysical. Under the LNA on log-abundances
$\underline{x}^{(c)}$, the total abundance $\Lambda^{(c)} = \sum_g
\lambda_g^{(c)}$ and the composition $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} =
\underline{\lambda}^{(c)} / \Lambda^{(c)}$ are in general correlated,
because both are functionals of the same Gaussian
$\underline{x}^{(c)}$.

We adopt the decoupled factorization here for two reasons. First, it
isolates the contribution of the LNM (the compositional model) from the
total-count model, allowing the chapter to focus exclusively on the
correlation structure that motivated the development. Second, in
practice the per-cell total count is dominated by capture efficiency
(the technical scaling factor introduced when mRNA molecules are
sampled into UMIs), which is approximately orthogonal to which genes a
cell expresses. The bias introduced by treating $u_T$ and
$\underline{\rho}$ as independent is therefore expected to be small for
the inferential targets of this work. A principled coupling of the two
---either by deriving the joint $(u_T, \underline{\rho})$ distribution
from the LNA directly, or by introducing a hierarchical link between
$r_T$ and $\underline{\mu}$---is left for future development.

### Joint distribution and the marginalization problem {#sec-lnm-joint}

The joint distribution of the observed counts $\underline{u}^{(c)}$, the
composition latent $\underline{z}^{(c)}$, and the total count $u_T^{(c)}$ for
a single cell is

$$
\pi(\underline{u}^{(c)},\, \underline{z}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\,
\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}}) =
\underbrace{
\pi(\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\,
\underline{\rho}^{(c)})
}_{\text{Multinomial}}
\times
\underbrace{
\pi(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
}_{\text{Multivariate Normal}},
$${#eq-lnm-joint}

where $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} = \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}^{(c)})$ is a
deterministic function of $\underline{z}^{(c)}$.

To obtain the marginal likelihood of the observed counts (integrating out the
latent composition), we would need to evaluate

$$
\pi(\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\, \underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}) =
\int_{\mathbb{R}^{G-1}} d\underline{z}^{(c)} \;
\underbrace{
\pi(\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\,
\text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}^{(c)}))
}_{\text{Multinomial}}
\times
\underbrace{
\pi(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
}_{\text{MVN}}.
$${#eq-lnm-marginal-integral}

### Why the integral has no closed form {#sec-lnm-no-closed-form}

It is instructive to compare @eq-lnm-marginal-integral with the corresponding
integral in the Dirichlet-Multinomial derivation. In @sec-dirichlet-multinomial,
the Dirichlet-Multinomial arose from

$$
\pi(\underline{u} \mid u_T, \underline{r}) =
\int_{\Delta^G} d\underline{\rho} \;
\underbrace{
\pi(\underline{u} \mid u_T, \underline{\rho})
}_{\text{Multinomial}}
\times
\underbrace{
\pi(\underline{\rho} \mid \underline{r})
}_{\text{Dirichlet}}.
$${#eq-lnm-dm-integral-recap}

That integral evaluated to a ratio of products of Gamma functions (see
@eq-joint-target-int-dirichlet-multinomial) because the Multinomial and
Dirichlet are **conjugate**: the product $\text{Mult} \times \text{Dir}$ has
the form $\prod_g \rho_g^{u_g + r_g - 1}$, which is the kernel of another
Dirichlet distribution, and the normalizing constant of that Dirichlet provides
the closed-form answer.

For the logistic-normal, the analogous product is

$$
\text{Mult}(\underline{u} \mid u_T,
\text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z})) \times
\mathcal{N}(\underline{z} \mid \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
\;\propto\;
\exp\!\left(
\sum_{g=1}^{G} u_g \log \rho_g(\underline{z})
- \frac{1}{2}
(\underline{z} - \underline{\mu})^\top
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}
(\underline{z} - \underline{\mu})
\right),
$${#eq-lnm-integrand}

where $\rho_g(\underline{z})$ is the nonlinear softmax function of
$\underline{z}$ (from @eq-lnm-alr-inverse-first and
@eq-lnm-alr-inverse-last). The first term in the exponent---the multinomial
log-likelihood---is a **nonlinear** function of $\underline{z}$ (it involves
the log-softmax), and the second term is a **quadratic** function of
$\underline{z}$. The product of an exponential-of-nonlinear with an
exponential-of-quadratic does not reduce to any standard family, and the
integral over $\underline{z}$ has no known closed form.

This is the fundamental difference between the Dirichlet-Multinomial and the
Logistic-Normal Multinomial: **conjugacy is lost**. The price of a richer
correlation structure is the need for approximate inference over the per-cell
latent $\underline{z}^{(c)}$.

## Full Bayesian model {#sec-lnm-full-model}

Combining the total count model with the logistic-normal composition model
across all cells, the complete generative model for the observed UMI count
matrix $\underline{\underline{U}}$ is

$$
\pi(\underline{\underline{U}},\,
\{\underline{z}^{(c)}\}_{c=1}^{C} \mid
r_T, \hat{p}, \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}) =
\prod_{c=1}^{C} \left[
    \pi(u_T^{(c)} \mid r_T, \hat{p})
    \times
    \pi(\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},
    \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}^{(c)}))
    \times
    \pi(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{\mu},
    \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
\right],
$${#eq-lnm-full-joint}

with priors on the model parameters:

$$
r_T \sim \pi(r_T), \qquad
\hat{p} \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_p, \beta_p),
$${#eq-lnm-priors-total}

$$
\underline{\mu} \sim
\mathcal{N}(\underline{0}, \sigma_\mu^2 \underline{\underline{I}}),
\qquad
\underline{\underline{W}} \sim \pi(\underline{\underline{W}}),
\qquad
\underline{d} \sim \pi(\underline{d}).
$${#eq-lnm-priors-composition}

The posterior of interest is

$$
\pi(r_T, \hat{p}, \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}},
\{\underline{z}^{(c)}\}_{c=1}^{C} \mid
\underline{\underline{U}})
\propto
\pi(\underline{\underline{U}},\,
\{\underline{z}^{(c)}\}_{c=1}^{C} \mid
r_T, \hat{p}, \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
\times \pi(r_T, \hat{p}, \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}).
$${#eq-lnm-posterior}

Note the distinction between two types of latent variables:

- **Population parameters** ($r_T$, $\hat{p}$, $\underline{\mu}$,
  $\underline{\underline{W}}$, $\underline{d}$): shared across all cells,
  analogous to $\underline{r}$ and $\hat{p}$ in the standard model.
- **Per-cell latents** ($\underline{z}^{(c)}$ for $c = 1, \ldots, C$): the
  composition of each individual cell in log-ratio space. These play the role
  that $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} \sim \text{Dir}(\underline{r})$ plays in the
  DM model, except that they could not be marginalized analytically and must
  be inferred.

## Inference strategies {#sec-lnm-inference}

The loss of conjugacy means that the per-cell latent
$\underline{z}^{(c)}$ cannot be marginalized out analytically. Several
strategies exist for handling this integral within the SCRIBE variational
inference framework. We present them in order of increasing approximation,
discussing the trade-offs of each.

### Strategy A: Explicit per-cell latent variable {#sec-lnm-strategy-a}

The most direct approach is to treat each $\underline{z}^{(c)}$ as an explicit
latent variable in the probabilistic model and provide a variational posterior
for it.

**Model.** In NumPyro notation, the model samples $\underline{z}^{(c)}$ inside
a cell plate:

$$
\underline{z}^{(c)} \sim
\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}),
\quad c = 1, \ldots, C,
$${#eq-lnm-strategy-a-prior}

and then observes $\underline{u}^{(c)}$ conditioned on
$\text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}^{(c)})$ via the Multinomial.

**Guide.** The variational guide provides an approximate posterior
$q(\underline{z}^{(c)})$ for each cell. Two sub-strategies are possible:

**(A1) Per-cell variational parameters.** Each cell has its own variational
mean $\underline{m}^{(c)}$ and (diagonal or low-rank) variational covariance.
The number of variational parameters scales as $O(C \times (G-1))$, which for
$C = 100{,}000$ and $G = 20{,}000$ yields $\sim 2 \times 10^9$ parameters.
This is large but manageable with modern hardware, especially since the
per-cell parameters are only $(G-1)$-dimensional vectors (or low-rank
structured).

**(A2) Amortized inference.** An encoder network
$f_\phi(\underline{u}^{(c)}) \to (\underline{m}_\phi(\underline{u}^{(c)}),\,
\underline{s}_\phi^2(\underline{u}^{(c)}))$ maps the observed counts to the
parameters of a diagonal Gaussian variational posterior

$$
q_\phi(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)}) =
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\underline{m}_\phi(\underline{u}^{(c)}),\,
\text{diag}(\underline{s}_\phi^2(\underline{u}^{(c)}))
\right).
$${#eq-lnm-strategy-a2-encoder}

This reduces the variational parameter count to the size of the encoder
network (independent of $C$) and naturally generalizes to held-out cells.

**Why a diagonal $q_\phi$ suffices despite correlated genes.** That
$q_\phi$ is diagonal does *not* mean the model has lost cross-gene
correlations. Those correlations live in the **prior**
$\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu}, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$, whose
low-rank covariance $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d})$ is a population parameter learned from the
data. The encoder's only job is to locate each cell's posterior mean in
log-ratio space and a diagonal width around it; the KL term in the ELBO
ties the spread of those per-cell posteriors back to the population
covariance, so $\underline{\underline{W}}$ and $\underline{d}$ receive a
direct gradient from the data even though the per-cell $q_\phi$ is
factorized. A diagonal posterior is therefore not a model of independent
genes---it is the natural amortized approximation to a Gaussian whose
*structured* correlations are encoded once, globally, in the prior.

**Closed-form Gaussian--Gaussian KL.** Because both $q_\phi(\underline{z}^{(c)}
\mid \underline{u}^{(c)})$ and the prior $\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ are Gaussian, the KL term in the ELBO has
a closed form:

$$
\text{KL}\!\left(
q_\phi(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)})
\;\big\|\;
\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
\right) =
\frac{1}{2} \left[
\text{tr}\!\left(\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}\,
\text{diag}(\underline{s}_\phi^2)\right)
+ (\underline{m}_\phi - \underline{\mu})^\top
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}
(\underline{m}_\phi - \underline{\mu})
- (G - 1)
+ \log \frac{|\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}|}{\prod_{g=1}^{G-1} s_{\phi,g}^2}
\right].
$${#eq-lnm-closed-form-kl}

Both $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1}$ and $\log|\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}|$
are computed in $O(Gk^2 + k^3)$ via the Woodbury identity
(@eq-lnm-woodbury) and the matrix determinant lemma (@eq-lnm-log-det).
Only the multinomial expectation
$\left\langle \log \text{Mult}(\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},
\text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}^{(c)})) \right\rangle_{q_\phi}$ requires reparameterized
sampling. This is a meaningful variance-reduction win and should be built
into the loss from the outset rather than letting the framework sample
the KL term too. Under the linear-decoder reparameterization developed
in @sec-lnm-linear-vae-equivalence, this expression collapses further to
the canonical Kingma--Welling VAE KL between two diagonal Gaussians
(@eq-lnm-vae-standard-kl), with $O(k)$ cost per cell and no Woodbury
precompute required.

**Mapping onto SCRIBE's existing infrastructure.** SCRIBE has *two*
amortization primitives that should not be confused:

- The `AmortizedGuide` family is designed to amortize a per-data-point
  *parameter* whose prior is fixed at the model level (e.g., the
  cell-specific capture probability $p_{\text{capture}}^{(c)}$).
- The `VAELatentGuide` family (with its `GaussianEncoder`,
  `MultiHeadDecoder`, and `GaussianLatentSpec`) is designed for per-cell
  *latent variables* that are sampled in the guide and consumed downstream
  in the model.

The LNM matches the second pattern, so the natural fit is
`VAELatentGuide` with a Gaussian latent of dimension $G - 1$. Mapped
naively, this would require two extensions to the existing VAE pipeline:

1. The prior on the latent would be a *learnable, low-rank* Gaussian
   $\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{W}}\,
   \underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \text{diag}(\underline{d}))$, with
   $\underline{\mu}$, $\underline{\underline{W}}$, $\underline{d}$
   registered as ordinary population parameters (each carrying its own
   variational guide). The default `GaussianLatentSpec` assumes a fixed
   $\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}})$ prior and
   would need to be generalized to read its prior parameters from the
   model graph.
2. The "decoder" path would be the deterministic map
   $\underline{z} \mapsto \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}) =
   \underline{\rho}$, fed straight into the multinomial likelihood. With
   no neural decoder to train, the simplest implementation would be a
   passthrough that lets the
   `LogisticNormalMultinomialLikelihood` consume $\underline{z}^{(c)}$
   directly and apply the inverse ALR internally.

Both extensions can be avoided by adopting an equivalent
reparameterization of the LNM as a linear-decoder VAE with a
$k$-dimensional bottleneck. Under that reparameterization, the standard
$\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_k)$ prior and a
single $\text{Dense}(G-1)$ decoder layer with identity activation
recover the same generative model with no changes to the existing VAE
infrastructure. We develop this view in
@sec-lnm-linear-vae-equivalence and adopt it as the implementation path.

**Advantages:**

- No approximation of the likelihood: the ELBO is a valid lower bound.
- Standard SVI machinery applies directly; mini-batching over cells is
  straightforward.
- The reparameterization trick applies to the Gaussian latent, enabling
  low-variance gradient estimates.

**Disadvantages:**

- Large number of latent variables (one $(G-1)$-vector per cell).
- The ELBO requires evaluating the multinomial log-probability for each cell,
  which involves the log-softmax---a $O(G)$ operation per cell.

### Strategy B: Laplace approximation {#sec-lnm-strategy-b}

Instead of maintaining per-cell variational parameters, we can approximate the
posterior $\pi(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)}, \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ using a Laplace approximation centered at
the mode.

**Procedure.** For each cell $c$, find the MAP estimate

$$
\underline{z}^{(c)}_\star = \arg\max_{\underline{z}} \left[
\log \pi(\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},
\text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}))
+ \log \pi(\underline{z} \mid \underline{\mu},
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})
\right],
$${#eq-lnm-laplace-map}

and approximate the posterior as

$$
q(\underline{z}^{(c)}) =
\mathcal{N}(\underline{z}^{(c)}_\star,\,
\underline{\underline{H}}^{(c)^{-1}}),
$${#eq-lnm-laplace-approx}

where $\underline{\underline{H}}^{(c)}$ is the Hessian of the negative
log-posterior at $\underline{z}^{(c)}_\star$.

**Advantages:**

- Deterministic: no sampling noise in the gradient estimates.
- The Hessian has a known structure (sum of multinomial Fisher information and
  prior precision), enabling efficient computation.

**Disadvantages:**

- Requires a per-cell inner optimization loop (Newton's method or similar).
- The quality of the approximation depends on how well the posterior is
  approximated by a Gaussian, which may be poor for low-count cells.
- Not naturally compatible with mini-batching: the inner optimization must
  converge for each cell in the mini-batch.

### Strategy C: Pólya-Gamma augmentation {#sec-lnm-strategy-c}

The logistic-normal-multinomial has a connection to the Pólya-Gamma
augmentation scheme developed for logistic regression models. By introducing
auxiliary Pólya-Gamma random variables, the nonlinear log-softmax term in
@eq-lnm-integrand can be replaced by a quadratic form in $\underline{z}$,
making the conditional posterior of $\underline{z}$ Gaussian.

This strategy is well-developed for binary and multi-class logistic regression
but becomes computationally expensive for large $G$, as it requires one
Pólya-Gamma variable per gene per cell. We mention it for completeness but do
not develop it further here.

### Strategy D: Moment-matched Dirichlet proxy {#sec-lnm-strategy-d}

A pragmatic alternative is to moment-match the logistic-normal to a Dirichlet
distribution and use the Dirichlet-Multinomial closed form for the likelihood,
while extracting cross-gene correlations from the logistic-normal parameters
$\underline{\mu}$ and $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ after fitting.

**Procedure.** Given $\underline{\mu}$ and
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$, compute the mean and variance of each
composition component under the logistic-normal (by Monte Carlo or
approximation), then find Dirichlet concentrations
$\underline{r}_{\text{proxy}}$ that match these moments.

**Advantages:**

- Retains the closed-form DM likelihood: no per-cell latent variables.
- Compatible with the entire existing SCRIBE inference pipeline.
- Computational cost unchanged from the standard model.

**Disadvantages:**

- **The likelihood gradient on $\underline{\underline{W}}$ is structurally
  zero.** Because the moment-matched DM is parameterized only by the
  concentrations $\underline{r}_{\text{proxy}}$, which depend solely on the
  *marginal* moments of the logistic-normal (not on the off-diagonal entries
  of $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$), the likelihood term in the ELBO
  carries no information about the cross-gene correlation factor
  $\underline{\underline{W}}$. Any signal in $\underline{\underline{W}}$
  comes purely from prior regularization, not from the data---which puts
  this strategy in essentially the same regime that already produced the
  null cluster-recovery results on synthetic data with the standard DM
  model. Strategy D therefore inherits the very problem the LNM was
  designed to fix.
- The quality of the Dirichlet approximation depends on how close the
  logistic-normal is to a Dirichlet---which is precisely the setting where
  the logistic-normal is most needed (i.e., when correlations are strong).

### Summary of inference strategies

| Strategy | Per-cell latents | Likelihood | Gradient quality | Scalability |
|----------|-----------------|------------|------------------|-------------|
| A1: Explicit (per-cell) | $O(C(G{-}1))$ params | Exact | Reparameterized | Medium |
| A2: Explicit (amortized) | Encoder network | Exact | Reparameterized | High |
| B: Laplace | None (point estimate) | Approximate | Deterministic | Medium |
| C: Pólya-Gamma | $O(CG)$ auxiliary | Exact (augmented) | Gibbs | Low for large $G$ |
| D: DM proxy | None | Approximate (DM) | Standard | High |

The choice between strategies depends on the balance between statistical
fidelity and computational cost. Strategy A2 (amortized) is the most
natural fit for SCRIBE's existing architecture, as the framework already
supports VAE-style amortized inference for per-cell latents through the
`VAELatentGuide` family (see the implementation notes in
@sec-lnm-strategy-a). Strategy D is the least invasive but, as noted
above, its likelihood gradient on $\underline{\underline{W}}$ is
structurally zero, so it cannot recover correlations from data---it only
retains the prior on $\underline{\underline{W}}$. Strategies B and C are
included for completeness; neither is competitive with A2 at the dataset
scales of interest.

## Equivalence to a linear-decoder VAE {#sec-lnm-linear-vae-equivalence}

The Strategy A2 picture developed in @sec-lnm-strategy-a treats the
per-cell latent $\underline{z}^{(c)}$ as living directly in
$\mathbb{R}^{G-1}$ (ALR space), with a learnable low-rank Gaussian prior
$\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\, \underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \text{diag}(\underline{d}))$. This view
is mathematically clean but operationally costly: the encoder must output
$2(G-1)$ variational parameters per cell, the KL involves the Woodbury
inverse of the population covariance, and the inference framework must
support a learnable prior on its latent.

There is an equivalent reparameterized view---fully isomorphic to the
original LNM under a deterministic affine map---in which the model is a
*linear-decoder VAE* with a $k$-dimensional bottleneck. In this view, the
rank-$k$ structure of $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ migrates from "a
learnable prior parameter on a $(G-1)$-dimensional latent" to "a
learnable linear decoder mapping a $k$-dimensional latent to ALR space".
The two formulations are exactly the same generative model, but the
second is dramatically cheaper to implement and maps onto pre-existing
VAE infrastructure essentially as-is.

### Reparameterizing the rank-$k$ Gaussian {#sec-lnm-reparam}

The low-rank Gaussian on $\underline{z}$ admits the affine
reparameterization

$$
\underline{h} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_k),
\qquad
\underline{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_{G-1}),
\qquad
\underline{z} = \underline{\mu} + \underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{h}
+ \text{diag}(\sqrt{\underline{d}})\,\underline{\epsilon},
$${#eq-lnm-vae-reparam}

with $\underline{h}$ and $\underline{\epsilon}$ independent. Pushing this
affine map through the standard rule for transformations of Gaussians
yields

$$
\langle \underline{z} \rangle = \underline{\mu},
\qquad
\text{Cov}(\underline{z}) =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{I}}_k\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top
+ \text{diag}(\sqrt{\underline{d}})\,\underline{\underline{I}}_{G-1}\,
\text{diag}(\sqrt{\underline{d}})
=
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \text{diag}(\underline{d}).
$${#eq-lnm-vae-marginal}

So the marginal distribution of $\underline{z}$ in @eq-lnm-vae-reparam is
identical to the prior $\mathcal{N}(\underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d}))$ used in @eq-lnm-cell-latent. This is just
the standard "factor model" decomposition of a low-rank Gaussian:
$\underline{h}$ carries the rank-$k$ shared variation, and
$\underline{\epsilon}$ carries the gene-independent residuals.

### The linear-decoder VAE form {#sec-lnm-linear-vae-form}

Using @eq-lnm-vae-reparam, the LNM generative process from
@sec-lnm-generative becomes

$$
u_T^{(c)} \sim \text{NB}(r_T,\, \hat{p}),
$${#eq-lnm-vae-uT}

$$
\underline{h}^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_k),
\qquad
\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_{G-1}),
$${#eq-lnm-vae-latents}

$$
\underline{z}^{(c)} = \underline{\mu} + \underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{h}^{(c)}
+ \text{diag}(\sqrt{\underline{d}})\,\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)},
$${#eq-lnm-vae-decode}

$$
\underline{\rho}^{(c)} = \text{ALR}^{-1}(\underline{z}^{(c)}),
\qquad
\underline{u}^{(c)} \mid u_T^{(c)},\, \underline{\rho}^{(c)} \sim
\text{Multinomial}(u_T^{(c)},\, \underline{\rho}^{(c)}).
$${#eq-lnm-vae-multinomial}

Equation @eq-lnm-vae-decode is precisely the forward pass of a single
linear neural-network layer: the matrix $\underline{\underline{W}}$ plays
the role of the **kernel** (weights) and $\underline{\mu}$ plays the role
of the **bias**, with input $\underline{h}^{(c)}$ and output
$\underline{z}^{(c)}$. The diagonal-$\underline{d}$ component appears as
additive Gaussian noise at the layer's output. The latent
$\underline{h}^{(c)}$ is the bottleneck of the VAE, with dimension $k$
rather than $G - 1$.

The variational posterior is also reparameterized. Instead of
approximating a posterior over $\underline{z}^{(c)} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$,
we approximate a posterior over $\underline{h}^{(c)} \in \mathbb{R}^k$:

$$
q_\phi(\underline{h}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)}) =
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\underline{m}_\phi(\underline{u}^{(c)}),\,
\text{diag}(\underline{s}_\phi^2(\underline{u}^{(c)}))
\right),
\quad \underline{m}_\phi,\,\underline{s}_\phi^2 \in \mathbb{R}^k.
$${#eq-lnm-vae-encoder}

The variational nuisance noise $\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)}$ requires no
posterior: its variational distribution is set equal to its prior
$\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_{G-1})$, which
contributes zero to the KL while still allowing the diagonal
$\underline{d}$ to be identified through its effect on the marginal
multinomial likelihood.

**A subtle gain in posterior expressivity.** A diagonal posterior on
$\underline{h}^{(c)} \in \mathbb{R}^k$ is *not* equivalent to a diagonal
posterior on $\underline{z}^{(c)} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$. The deterministic
linear decoder propagates the diagonal posterior on $\underline{h}^{(c)}$
into a structured low-rank-plus-diagonal posterior on $\underline{z}^{(c)}$:

$$
\text{Cov}(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)}) =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,
\text{diag}(\underline{s}_\phi^2(\underline{u}^{(c)}))\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top
+ \text{diag}(\underline{d}).
$${#eq-lnm-vae-induced-posterior}

This is strictly more expressive than the diagonal $q_\phi(\underline{z}
\mid \underline{u})$ posited by the direct $(G-1)$-dimensional
formulation of @sec-lnm-strategy-a. The VAE reparameterization
therefore delivers two distinct improvements simultaneously: it
*reduces* the encoder's output dimension from $2(G-1)$ to $2k$ (a
computational win), *and* it *upgrades* the implied posterior on
$\underline{z}^{(c)}$ from diagonal to rank-$k$-plus-diagonal (a
statistical win). Both advantages stem from pushing the rank-$k$
structure from "a learnable prior on a $(G-1)$-dim latent" to "a
deterministic linear decoder from a $k$-dim latent".

### Closed-form KL is now the standard VAE KL {#sec-lnm-vae-kl}

Because the reparameterized prior on $\underline{h}^{(c)}$ is the
standard isotropic Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\,
\underline{\underline{I}}_k)$, the KL term @eq-lnm-closed-form-kl
collapses to the standard VAE KL between two diagonal Gaussians:

$$
\text{KL}\!\left(
q_\phi(\underline{h}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)})
\;\big\|\;
\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_k)
\right) =
\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{k}
\left(
s_{\phi,j}^2(\underline{u}^{(c)})
+ m_{\phi,j}^2(\underline{u}^{(c)})
- 1
- \log s_{\phi,j}^2(\underline{u}^{(c)})
\right).
$${#eq-lnm-vae-standard-kl}

This is the canonical Kingma--Welling VAE KL, evaluated in $O(k)$ per
cell. The Woodbury inverse and matrix-determinant lemma machinery from
@eq-lnm-woodbury and @eq-lnm-log-det are no longer needed in the loss:
they are absorbed into the definition of the linear decoder.

### Cost reduction {#sec-lnm-vae-cost}

Compared to the direct $(G-1)$-dimensional formulation of
@sec-lnm-strategy-a, the linear-decoder form is uniformly cheaper:

| Quantity | Direct $(G-1)$-dim form | Linear-decoder VAE |
|----------|-------------------------|--------------------|
| Encoder output dimension | $2(G-1)$ | $2k$ |
| Per-cell latent dimension | $G - 1$ | $k$ |
| KL per cell | $O(Gk + k^2)$ via @eq-lnm-closed-form-kl | $O(k)$ via @eq-lnm-vae-standard-kl |
| Per-batch precompute | $O(Gk^2 + k^3)$ for Woodbury factors | None |
| Learnable prior in inference framework | Yes | No (prior is fixed $\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_k)$) |
| Decoder structure | Identity / passthrough | Single $\text{Dense}(G-1)$ linear layer |

For $G = 20{,}000$, $k = 128$, $H = 512$ (encoder hidden width), and
$B = 256$ cells per mini-batch, the encoder output layer shrinks from
$\sim 2 \times 10^4$ to $\sim 2.5 \times 10^2$ units. The total ELBO cost
per gradient step drops from $O(BGH + BGk + Gk^2 + k^3)$ to
$O(BGH + BGk + Bk)$, a saving driven primarily by the elimination of the
per-batch Woodbury precompute and the shrinking of the closed-form KL
from $O(Gk)$ to $O(k)$ per cell.

### The diagonal $\underline{d}$ in this view {#sec-lnm-vae-d}

The reparameterization @eq-lnm-vae-reparam admits two practical regimes
for the diagonal residual noise. A subtle but important consequence of
the VAE formulation is that the marginal covariance of
$\underline{z}^{(c)}$ never has to be inverted: the ELBO involves only
the standard Gaussian prior $\mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\,
\underline{\underline{I}}_k)$ on $\underline{h}^{(c)}$ and the
Multinomial likelihood on $\underline{u}^{(c)}$, neither of which
requires $\text{Cov}(\underline{z}^{(c)})^{-1}$ or
$\log |\text{Cov}(\underline{z}^{(c)})|$. The marginal covariance of
$\underline{z}^{(c)}$ is therefore allowed to be **singular** without
any numerical penalty---a freedom we did not have in the direct
$(G-1)$-dim formulation of @sec-lnm-strategy-a, which evaluates
$\log \mathcal{N}(\underline{z} \mid \underline{\mu},\,
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}})$ as a density and therefore requires
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ to be positive definite.

**Strictly low-rank mode.** Drop the residual-noise term
$\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)}$ entirely (equivalently, set $\underline{d}
= \underline{0}$). The generative path becomes the deterministic linear
decoder

$$
\underline{z}^{(c)} = \underline{\mu} + \underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{h}^{(c)},
\qquad \underline{h}^{(c)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underline{0},\, \underline{\underline{I}}_k),
$${#eq-lnm-vae-low-rank}

with marginal covariance *exactly*
$\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{\underline{W}}^\top$. This
covariance is rank-deficient---rank $k$ in an ambient $(G-1)$-dimensional
space---but, by the argument above, this is harmless: no part of the
loss inverts it. Compositions in ALR space genuinely live on a
$k$-dimensional affine manifold up to multinomial sampling noise, and
the model is internally consistent. This is the simplest and cheapest
mode: no per-cell stochastic node beyond $\underline{h}^{(c)}$, no
encoder output for residual noise, and the encoder still emits only
$2k$ variational parameters per cell. It is sufficient whenever the
$k$-dimensional bottleneck already explains essentially all of the
compositional variance.

**Learned mode.** Treat $\underline{d}$ as a positive learnable per-gene
parameter, with $\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)}$ a per-cell stochastic node
whose variational posterior is set equal to its prior. The KL
contribution from $\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)}$ is exactly zero, so the
gradient on $\underline{d}$ comes entirely from the multinomial
log-likelihood through the reparameterized path

$$
\underline{z}^{(c)} = \underline{\mu} +
\underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{h}^{(c)} +
\text{diag}(\sqrt{\underline{d}})\,\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)}.
$${#eq-lnm-vae-learned}

This recovers the full $\underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top + \text{diag}(\underline{d})$ marginal of
@eq-lnm-vae-marginal at the cost of one additional $(G-1)$-vector of
stochastic noise per cell---cheap, since $\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)}$
has no posterior and no encoder output. The mode is appropriate when
the data exhibits gene-specific compositional fluctuations that the
$k$-rank shared factor cannot absorb.

A *fixed*, non-zero diagonal (i.e., $\underline{d} = d_0\,
\underline{1}$ with $d_0$ chosen rather than learned) is also possible,
but it requires sampling $\underline{\epsilon}^{(c)}$ exactly as in the
learned mode and trades the gain of a learnable per-gene scale for a
single hyperparameter. In the VAE form there is no numerical
justification for such a fixed jitter---it cannot serve the
positive-definiteness role it played in the direct formulation---so we
do not develop it as a separate mode.

In both modes, the rank-$k$ structure---the part of the covariance that
captures cross-gene correlations and is the object of scientific
interest---lives entirely in the decoder weights
$\underline{\underline{W}}$.

### Comparison to nonlinear-decoder VAEs {#sec-lnm-vae-vs-scvi}

A natural and important question is what we lose by insisting on a
*linear* decoder, given that nonlinear-decoder VAEs (most prominently
scVI) have proven highly effective at modeling single-cell counts.

A linear decoder gives the marginal covariance an exact, globally valid,
low-rank-plus-diagonal form. A nonlinear decoder $f_\theta : \mathbb{R}^k
\to \mathbb{R}^{G-1}$ does not. Under a nonlinear $f_\theta$, the
induced covariance of $\underline{z}^{(c)}$ at a given posterior mean
$\underline{\bar{z}}^{(c)}$ is approximately

$$
\text{Cov}(\underline{z}^{(c)} \mid \underline{u}^{(c)}) \approx
\underline{\underline{J}}_{f_\theta}(\underline{\bar{z}}^{(c)})\,
\underline{\underline{J}}_{f_\theta}(\underline{\bar{z}}^{(c)})^\top
+ \cdots,
$${#eq-lnm-nonlinear-cov}

where $\underline{\underline{J}}_{f_\theta}$ is the Jacobian of the
decoder. This Jacobian is **state-dependent**---it varies across the
latent space---so there is no single matrix one can extract from a
fitted nonlinear-decoder VAE that plays the role of
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$. A nonlinear decoder buries the
cross-gene correlations into its weights *implicitly*; a linear decoder
makes them an *explicit* and clusterable object $\underline{\underline{W}}$.

This is precisely the property that the synthetic-validation null result
(see @sec-lnm-dirichlet-limitations) demanded: an explicit, generative
correlation factor that downstream analyses can act on directly. Trading
capacity for interpretability---the linear-decoder LNM cannot model
curved compositional manifolds in log-ratio space, but it makes
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ a first-class object---is the correct
trade-off for the present application. The natural sequel is a
*nonlinear* LNM whose decoder is an MLP rather than a single
$\text{Dense}$ layer; this strictly subsumes the linear case in capacity
at the cost of giving up the closed-form $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$,
and is left to future work.

### Why we adopt this view for implementation {#sec-lnm-vae-adoption}

The linear-decoder VAE form is mathematically identical to the rank-$k$
LNM developed in @sec-lnm-model. There is no theoretical content in
switching between the two views; they are the same generative model,
written differently. We adopt the linear-decoder view because:

1. **It is computationally cheaper.** Encoder output $2k$ vs.\
   $2(G-1)$; KL is the standard VAE KL; no per-batch Woodbury
   precompute.
2. **It maps cleanly onto pre-existing VAE infrastructure.** The model
   is a standard linear-decoder VAE plus a multinomial output likelihood
   with an inverse-ALR transformation on the decoder output; the
   `VAELatentGuide` family in SCRIBE supports this without further
   extension.
3. **It makes the rank of the cross-gene covariance an explicit knob.**
   The bottleneck dimension $k$ *is* the rank of the population
   covariance; choosing $k$ is now a single hyperparameter selecting how
   much shared compositional variation the model can express.
4. **It clarifies the relationship to the broader VAE literature.** The
   LNM is, in this view, a linear analogue of scVI with a multinomial
   likelihood and an inverse-ALR transformation in place of scVI's
   softmax-with-NB factorization. This positions the model concretely on
   the spectrum of existing single-cell generative models.

The remaining sections of this chapter were developed in the direct
$(G-1)$-dimensional language for clarity; readers implementing the model
should mentally translate to @eq-lnm-vae-reparam through
@eq-lnm-vae-multinomial above.

## Connection to the existing SCRIBE framework {#sec-lnm-connections}

### Relationship to the normalization chapter {#sec-lnm-normalization-connection}

In @sec-normalization, we described how a logistic-normal distribution is fit
**post hoc** to the posterior predictive compositions
$\underline{\rho}^{(s)} \sim \text{Dir}(\underline{r}^{(s)})$ obtained from
the standard model. This post-hoc fitting captures the correlations induced by
variation in $\underline{r}$ across posterior samples, but it is a
**downstream summary**, not part of the generative model.

The logistic-normal multinomial model proposed here inverts this relationship:
the logistic-normal is the **generative prior** on compositions, and the
correlations are **parameters to be inferred**, not summaries to be computed
after the fact. This distinction is crucial---by embedding the logistic-normal
in the generative model, the likelihood function is sensitive to the
correlation structure, and the inference procedure is directly incentivized to
recover it from the data.

### Relationship to the Gaussianity diagnostics {#sec-lnm-gaussianity-connection}

The Gaussianity diagnostics developed in @sec-diffexp-gaussianity test whether
the posterior CLR coordinates are well-approximated by a Gaussian distribution.
Under the current DM model, this is an empirical question: the CLR coordinates
of Dirichlet draws are not Gaussian in general, but become approximately so
when the concentrations $\underline{r}$ are large (the central limit theorem
for the Dirichlet).

Under the logistic-normal model, **Gaussianity in ALR (and hence CLR) space is
exact by construction**. The Jarque-Bera diagnostic from
@sec-diffexp-gaussianity would yield $\text{JB}_g \approx 0$ for all genes,
confirming that the parametric differential expression path is always valid.
This means the fast $O(kD)$ parametric DE pipeline from the same section can
be used without qualification, and the CLR difference

$$
\Delta_{g,\text{CLR}} =
z_{g,\text{CLR}}^{(A)} - z_{g,\text{CLR}}^{(B)}
$${#eq-lnm-de-delta}

is exactly Gaussian with mean and variance directly computable from
$\underline{\mu}_A$, $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_A$,
$\underline{\mu}_B$, $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}_B$.

### Relationship to the hierarchical $p_g$ model {#sec-lnm-hierarchical-connection}

The hierarchical gene-specific $p_g$ model from @sec-hierarchical-p addressed
a different limitation of the standard model: it allowed the success
probability to vary across genes, breaking the DM factorization but enabling
the data to determine the degree of gene-to-gene variation in $p_g$.

The logistic-normal model is complementary: it addresses the
**compositional correlation structure** rather than the **total-count
model**. In principle, the two extensions can be combined---gene-specific
$p_g$ for the total count and a logistic-normal for the composition---but
they do *not* compose cleanly. Under the hierarchical $p_g$ model the
per-cell composition $\underline{\rho}^{(c)}$ is induced by the joint draw
$\lambda_g^{(c)} \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, p_g/(1-p_g))$ followed by
normalization $\underline{\rho}^{(c)} = \underline{\lambda}^{(c)} /
\lambda_T^{(c)}$ (see @sec-hier-gamma-sampling), so $\underline{\rho}^{(c)}$
is *not* a free parameter that can simply be replaced by an independent
logistic-normal draw. A principled combination would either (a) couple the
LNM compositional prior to a separate scalar total-count model with a
single $\hat{p}$ (losing per-gene over-dispersion in $u_T$), or (b)
introduce a fresh generative path that models cell- and gene-level
over-dispersion *jointly* in log-ratio space. We defer this design
question to a follow-up; in the present chapter we work with the
shared-$\hat{p}$ total-count model from @sec-lnm-model.

## Computational considerations {#sec-lnm-computational}

### Low-rank structure {#sec-lnm-low-rank}

For $G = 20{,}000$ genes, the full covariance matrix
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ would have $\sim 2 \times 10^8$ entries,
which is prohibitive. The low-rank factorization from @eq-lnm-low-rank-cov,

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}} =
\underline{\underline{W}}\,
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top +
\text{diag}(\underline{d}),
$${#eq-lnm-low-rank-repeated}

with $\underline{\underline{W}} \in \mathbb{R}^{(G-1) \times k}$ and
$\underline{d} \in \mathbb{R}^{G-1}_{>0}$, reduces the parameter count to
$(G-1)(k+1)$. For $k = 128$ (a typical rank for the SCRIBE low-rank guide),
this is $\sim 2.6 \times 10^6$ parameters---three orders of magnitude fewer
than the full covariance.

The low-rank structure also enables efficient sampling and density evaluation
via the Woodbury identity:

$$
\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}^{-1} =
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1} -
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}
\underline{\underline{W}}
\left(
\underline{\underline{I}}_k +
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}
\underline{\underline{W}}
\right)^{-1}
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1},
$${#eq-lnm-woodbury}

where $\underline{\underline{D}} = \text{diag}(\underline{d})$. This replaces
the $O(G^2)$ inversion with an $O(Gk^2 + k^3)$ computation, and the
log-determinant decomposes similarly via the matrix determinant lemma:

$$
\log |\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}| =
\log |\underline{\underline{D}}| +
\log |\underline{\underline{I}}_k +
\underline{\underline{W}}^\top
\underline{\underline{D}}^{-1}
\underline{\underline{W}}|.
$${#eq-lnm-log-det}

Both operations are $O(Gk^2)$, which is linear in the number of genes for
fixed $k$. NumPyro's `LowRankMultivariateNormal` distribution already
implements these efficient operations, and the SCRIBE
`LowRankLogisticNormal` distribution in `scribe.stats.distributions` wraps
them with the ALR transformation and Jacobian correction.

### Mini-batching compatibility {#sec-lnm-minibatch}

The product-over-cells structure of @eq-lnm-full-joint is naturally compatible
with stochastic variational inference via mini-batching. In each gradient step,
a random subset of cells is selected, and the ELBO contribution from those
cells is scaled by $C / B$ (where $B$ is the batch size). The per-cell latent
$\underline{z}^{(c)}$ is sampled only for cells in the current mini-batch.

For Strategy A2 (amortized inference), the encoder network maps each cell's
counts to variational parameters, so there is no need to maintain persistent
per-cell variational state---the encoder generalizes to all cells.

### Cost comparison

The table below compares the standard Dirichlet-Multinomial model with
the LNM under Strategy A2, in both its direct $(G-1)$-dimensional form
and its linear-decoder VAE reparameterization
(@sec-lnm-linear-vae-equivalence). $H$ denotes the encoder hidden
width.

| Operation | Standard DM | LNM A2 (direct $(G-1)$-dim) | LNM A2 (linear-decoder VAE) |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Likelihood per cell | $O(G)$ (DM formula) | $O(G)$ (log-softmax + Multinomial) | $O(G)$ (log-softmax + Multinomial) |
| Per-cell latent dim | --- | $G - 1$ | $k$ |
| Encoder output dim | --- | $2(G-1)$ | $2k$ |
| Latent sampling per cell | None (marginalized) | $O(G)$ (diagonal $q_\phi$ reparam) | $O(k)$ (diagonal $q_\phi$ reparam) |
| Linear decoder per cell | --- | --- | $O(Gk)$ ($\underline{\mu} + \underline{\underline{W}}\,\underline{h}$) |
| Population guide params | $O(Gk)$ over $\log\underline{r}$ | $O(Gk)$ over $\underline{\mu},\,\underline{\underline{W}},\,\underline{d}$ | $O(Gk)$ over decoder kernel/bias and $\underline{d}$ |
| Per-cell guide params | None | None (encoder weights, $O(GH)$) | None (encoder weights, $O(GH)$) |
| KL per cell | --- | $O(Gk + k^2)$ closed form (Woodbury) | $O(k)$ closed form (standard VAE KL) |
| Per-batch precompute | --- | $O(Gk^2 + k^3)$ Woodbury factors | None |
| Total ELBO per mini-batch | $O(BG + Gk)$ | $O(BGH + BGk + Gk^2 + k^3)$ | $O(BGH + BGk + Bk)$ |

Three things are worth flagging in this comparison. First, sampling from
$q_\phi$ is now $O(G)$ in the direct form (because $q_\phi$ is diagonal)
and $O(k)$ in the linear-decoder form (because the latent dimension is
$k$ rather than $G - 1$). The low-rank cost reappears only in the
direct-form KL evaluation; the linear-decoder form replaces it with the
$O(k)$ standard VAE KL. Second, the encoder is *not* a small add-on at
the absolute scale of $G \approx 20{,}000$: with input layer of size
$\sim G$ and a hidden width $H$, the encoder contributes $O(GH)$
parameters per layer, comparable in magnitude to a single column of the
population low-rank factor $\underline{\underline{W}}$. The encoder
*output* layer, however, shrinks from $2(G-1)$ to $2k$ in the
linear-decoder form---a meaningful saving since the output layer is the
parameter-count bottleneck of a wide encoder. Third, the linear-decoder
form has no per-batch Woodbury precompute, eliminating the $O(Gk^2 +
k^3)$ term entirely.

For $B = 256$, $G = 20{,}000$, $k = 128$, and $H = 512$, the encoder
forward pass contributes $\sim 2.6 \times 10^9$ operations per gradient
step. In the direct form the closed-form KL adds another $\sim 6.6
\times 10^8$ operations and the Woodbury precompute adds $\sim 2.6
\times 10^6$; in the linear-decoder form both terms are replaced by an
$O(Bk) = O(B \cdot 128) \approx 3 \times 10^4$ closed-form KL,
effectively free. The DM-side ELBO is recovered in the limit where the
encoder collapses to a constant (no per-cell adaptation) and
$\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ is moment-matched to a Dirichlet, which
is exactly Strategy D.

## What the logistic-normal model buys us {#sec-lnm-what-it-buys}

To conclude this theoretical development, we summarize what the logistic-normal
multinomial model provides over the standard Dirichlet-Multinomial:

1. **Explicit cross-gene correlations.** The covariance matrix
   $\underline{\underline{\Sigma}}$ (or its low-rank factors
   $\underline{\underline{W}}$, $\underline{d}$) directly parameterizes the
   correlation structure of gene compositions in log-ratio space. After
   fitting, $\underline{\underline{W}}$ can be used directly for clustering,
   dimensionality reduction, or pathway analysis---without the confounding
   effect of likelihood-induced coupling that plagues the variational guide's
   covariance in the standard model.

2. **Exact Gaussianity in log-ratio space.** The CLR coordinates are Gaussian
   by construction, validating the parametric differential expression pipeline
   without need for Jarque-Bera screening.

3. **Unified framework.** The logistic-normal model unifies the generative
   model (compositions), the normalization framework (@sec-normalization), and
   the differential expression framework (@sec-diffexp-gaussianity) under a
   single distributional assumption. Rather than fitting a logistic-normal
   post hoc to posterior predictive compositions, the logistic-normal
   is the model itself.

4. **Compositional correctness.** Like the Dirichlet-Multinomial, the
   logistic-normal multinomial respects the simplex constraint and the
   compositional nature of UMI counts. It does not require ad hoc
   normalization (such as CPM or log-normalization) because the composition is
   modeled probabilistically.

The cost is the loss of conjugacy and the need for approximate inference over
per-cell latent variables. The following sections develop the implementation
within SCRIBE's modular inference framework.

## Theoretical limitations and open questions {#sec-lnm-limitations}

The logistic-normal multinomial model resolves a structural limitation
of the Dirichlet-Multinomial (zero free correlation parameters) at the
cost of introducing approximations and modeling choices that are worth
making explicit. We collect the main theoretical caveats here, both as
a guide to where the model is expected to be reliable and as a record
of design decisions that may warrant revisiting in future work.

### The LNA approximation in low-count regimes {#sec-lnm-lim-lna}

The biophysical derivation of @sec-lnm-biophysical relies on two
approximations applied to the Chemical Langevin Equation: the linear
noise approximation (linearizing the drift around the deterministic
steady state) and the freezing of the noise amplitude at its
steady-state value (see @sec-lnm-bio-lna). Both approximations are
controlled by the same small parameter---the relative size of
fluctuations $\delta\underline{x}$ around the steady state in
log-abundance space---and both degrade for genes with very low molecular
counts.

In the low-count regime, the exact steady-state of an *independent*
birth-death process is Gamma-distributed, and the resulting compositional
prior is the Dirichlet of @sec-dirichlet-multinomial. The DM is therefore
the better marginal model for genes that are both lowly expressed *and*
weakly coupled to the rest of the network. The LNM should be understood
as the model of choice when (a) cross-gene correlations are the
inferential target, and (b) the genes carrying the relevant signal are
expressed at counts where the LNA is reliable. Pragmatically, this
covers the bulk of genes that drive cell-type structure in scRNA-seq,
but it does not cover every gene equally well.

### Identifiability of $\underline{\underline{W}}$ {#sec-lnm-lim-identifiability}
